prompt:
I'm putting a minesweeper board into our internal dashboard and the UI folks want the game logic as a pure library — no CLI, no classes with hidden state, just functions over immutable snapshots so React can diff them. Write minesweeper.ts (stdlib only, no npm) with this exact surface; test_minesweeper.ts is the acceptance contract and is off limits.

Boards come from fixture layout strings: lines of '.' (safe) and '*' (mine), all the same length; a trailing newline is fine. parseLayout(text) returns boolean[][] and throws Error for an empty layout, ragged lines, or any other character. newGame(layout) returns a Game — an object with rows, cols, mines, revealed, marks, status ('playing' | 'won' | 'lost') and boom (the [r, c] of the fatal mine, else null). adjacency(game, r, c) counts mines in the 8-neighborhood.

Purity is the whole point: reveal/mark/chord never modify their argument — they return a fresh Game — and any call that changes nothing (acting on a finished game, revealing an already-revealed or flagged cell, marking a revealed cell, chording anything unsatisfied) must return the EXACT SAME object, not a copy. Any coordinate off the board throws RangeError from every function that takes one.

mark(game, r, c) cycles a hidden cell none -> flag -> question -> none. reveal(game, r, c): a flag blocks the reveal (same-object no-op) but a question mark doesn't — revealing clears it. Revealing a mine sets status 'lost' and boom. Revealing a safe cell with adjacency 0 flood-fills: the connected zero region plus its numbered border, 8-connected; the flood never opens a flagged cell but does open (and unmark) question cells. When every safe cell is revealed the game is 'won' — flags have nothing to do with winning.

chord(game, r, c) is the reveal-around-a-satisfied-number move: legal only when the cell is already revealed, its adjacency is non-zero, and the number of FLAGGED neighbors equals that adjacency (question marks don't count). It then opens every neighbor that isn't revealed or flagged, scanning the 3x3 block in reading order (row-major, centre skipped); each safe open flood-fills as usual, and if an open hits a mine (someone flagged the wrong square) the game is lost on the spot with that neighbor as boom — later neighbors stay shut.

flagsRemaining(game) is mine count minus placed flags and may go negative. render(game) returns the rows joined with \n, no trailing newline: hidden '#', flag 'F', question '?', revealed cells '.' for zero else the digit. Once the game is lost the render tells the post-mortem: the boom cell is '!', every other mine shows '*' unless it was correctly flagged (stays 'F'), and a flag sitting on a safe cell becomes 'X'; everything else renders as normal.

node --test test_minesweeper.ts must pass as shipped.
